title: 'Luke says ok': Minister’s office aware of 'egregious' election message
author: Adam Holmes
fullText: It was the morning of July 3, 2025, and Tasmania was in the midst of another state election campaign.Out of the blue, participants in Tasmania's racing industry received a text message from Tasracing — a government-owned business.It spoke of the benefits of the Liberal Party's signature policy, a new state-owned insurance company called TasInsure."Tasracing is pleased to advise that TasInsure, a Tasmanian insurer that will be established by a Liberal Government if re-elected, will provide workers compensation insurance among its products that is expected to be applicable, subject to eligibility, for racing industry participants," it read."We will continue to keep you updated on developments as they relate to [the] insurance announcement, but believe it is a positive development that could assist participants with business operating costs."The SMS also landed with the then-opposition leader Dean Winter.The message had been sent out as a media release and published on the Tasracing website.Suspecting that caretaker conventions were being breached — due to government resources being used to promote a party-political message — Mr Winter contacted Tasracing chair Gene Phair, who quickly had the message pulled down.Racing Minister Jane Howlett used social media to share Tasracing's statement on TasInsure. (Facebook: Jane Howlett)Racing Minister Jane Howlett and Tasracing faced extensive questioning in parliament over how the message came to be.Dean Winter: I want to confirm with you that you and no-one in your office or the government was aware that the text message about TasInsure from Tasracing was going out?Jane Howlett: I can certainly tell you that I and no-one in my office was aware.Mr Winter: And no-one in the government was aware?Ms Howlett: I can't speak for every single person in government or who works in government offices, but certainly, I wasn't aware and no-one from my office was aware.Emails obtained by the ABC under right-to-information provisions show Ms Howlett's office was aware of the message before it was sent out.The new government business was announced during the July 2025 election as a response to rising insurance premiums. (ABC News: Kate Nickels)TasInsure had been announced by Premier Jeremy Rockliff on July 2.It was more of a concept than a fully mapped-out policy, but some industry bodies appeared to have more details than the public, including the workers' compensation aspect.This may have come via briefings, but no information on these could be discovered under right-to-information (RTI) requests.What did emerge, however, was that Tasracing started preparing communications to send out to its members that afternoon.The first written mention of this occurred at 4.48 pm, when public relations consultant Nicolas Turner — who handles Tasracing PR — emailed a draft of the message to Tasracing chief executive officer Andrew Jenkins.TasRacing CEO Andrew Jenkins oversaw TasInsure communications the day Mr Rockliff announced the policy. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)A minor change was made — adding a "subject to eligibility" line — and then it was ready by 6.16 pm.In sending the completed version to Mr Jenkins, Mr Turner added this comment to the top of his email."Luke says ok for tonight now," he wrote.The 'Luke' referenced is Luke Gaetani, senior advisor to Racing Minister Jane Howlett.The ABC understands that Mr Gaetani was made aware of the message as a standard courtesy, to ensure the relevant ministerial office is aware of upcoming communications to be sent out by a government business.There is no suggestion Mr Gaetani had input into the wording of the message.Mr Turner said the government is not involved in how Tasracing distributes its communications.The ABC sought further information regarding communications between the men, along with Ms Howlett, but none was found under RTI.On December 3, Ms Howlett was again asked about the matter.Janie Finlay (Labor): Did you or anyone from your office speak to anybody at Tasracing before their announcement on 3 July?Ms Howlett: I did not speak to anyone on 3 July. I don't believe anyone from my office did either.Tasracing boss takes fall for messageA government spokesperson said the government was not aware that Tasracing intended to issue a text message or social media post.They pointed out that the workers' compensation detail had been published earlier on July 2, by the Tasmanian Small Business Council.The correspondence shows a senior advisor to Jane Howlett was made aware of the potential breach of election conventions. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)Mr Jenkins faced a similar question in an estimates hearing.Mr Winter: Was anyone in the government or the Liberal Party made aware that this text message was going out?Mr Jenkins: Not to my knowledge.Caretaker conventions are intended to prevent government resources from being used for party-political purposes during election campaigns.Mr Jenkins took full responsibility for the text message."Ultimately, the decision to send the SMS that you are referring to resides with me," he told parliament in November."It was in no way intended as a political statement or endorsement."Ms Howlett earlier had to correct the record regarding her Facebook post linking to the Tasracing message.When questioned in parliament on December 3, she said she was unaware of the Tasracing communications until a week earlier, despite her Facebook post being made in July.Her post was then pointed out to her by Labor; it remains online today.The TasInsure statement was available on the Tasracing website but is now offline. (Tasracing)"Yes, it is my Facebook page, so I take full responsibility for the reposting of the Tasracing media release about TasInsure," Mr Howlett told parliament."I also want to make it clear, at no time did I direct the company to send out a text message or statements about the policy."Mr Rockliff was asked whether it was an "acceptable" use of Tasracing resources, to which he responded "no".Labor argued that Tasracing is listed as a state service agency under the code of conduct and so their resources cannot be used in a party-political way.The conventions read:"Online platforms, social media platforms or other electronic communications either hosted or managed by agencies, should remain apolitical during a caretaker period."Current Labor leader Josh Willie wrote to Mr Rockliff last year, calling for more action to be taken on what they described as an "egregious breach" of the conventions by Tasracing.They are just guidelines, however.Since then, government ministers have continued to say the matter has been dealt with.
summary: In the middle of another Tasmanian election campaign, the state's racing participants received a text message from a government body promoting a Liberal policy — an apparent breach of caretaker convention.
